title: What Is Visual Testing? History, Applications, and Terminology
narrationScript: Visual testing is the oldest method in nondestructive testing, and it is still the one we use the most. Long before there were x-ray tubes or ultrasonic flaw detectors, a craftsman judged a casting or a forging with nothing but trained eyes and good light. That instinct never went away. Today visual testing is usually the first method applied to a part and the only one applied to all of it, one hundred percent, every time. So our starting point is simple. We need to agree on what visual testing is, where it came from, where it is used, and the words we use to describe what we find. Let's define it plainly. Visual testing is the examination of a part using light and the eye, sometimes aided by simple tools like mirrors, magnifiers, and gauges, or by remote tools like borescopes and cameras, to judge the condition, the geometry, and the surface of that part. If it can be seen, directly or with an optical aid, it falls inside visual testing. The method reads what reaches the surface, so it is strongest on surface condition and shape, and on its own it tells us nothing about what is buried deep inside the metal. The history is worth knowing because it shapes how the method is treated. For most of human history, quality control was visual. Smiths and founders judged their work by eye, by sound, and by feel. When the industrial age arrived, steam boilers, railroads, and pressure vessels raised the stakes. Failures killed people, and that pressure produced the first written inspection codes and, eventually, formal qualification of inspectors. In the nineteen sixties the first recommended practice for qualifying nondestructive testing personnel appeared, and visual testing took its place as a recognized method alongside radiography, penetrant, and magnetic particle. Meanwhile the tools grew up. Hand magnifiers and mirrors gave way to rigid borescopes, then flexible fiber optics, then video probes with electronic image sensors, and now robotic crawlers, drones, and fully digital records. Where is it used? Almost everywhere metal is joined or a load is carried. In welding and fabrication, visual testing checks joint preparation, fit up, the in process welds, and the finished product. It is the front line for castings and forgings, for pressure vessels, boilers, piping, and storage tanks. Power plants rely on it, refineries and pipelines rely on it, and aerospace relies on it heavily, including borescope inspection deep inside running engines. Bridges, cranes, structural steel, ships, railcars, and automotive parts all get examined by eye. The same method serves new construction and in service work, which is to say it follows a part from raw material all the way through its working life. Now the part that trips up new inspectors, the vocabulary. These words are not interchangeable, and using them loosely will get you in trouble. A discontinuity is any interruption in the normal structure of the material, a void, a crack, an inclusion, a change in geometry. An indication is the evidence you actually see that suggests a discontinuity is present. Interpretation is deciding what that indication really is, whether it is relevant, nonrelevant, or false. Evaluation is the next step, judging a relevant indication against the acceptance criteria written in the code or specification. And only then comes the word defect. A defect is a discontinuity that has been evaluated and found rejectable. Say that to yourself until it is automatic. A discontinuity is not a defect. It earns the name defect only when the acceptance criteria say it must be rejected. One more point about terminology. The exact meaning of a term can shift between industries and between governing documents, so you always read the definitions in the procedure and specification that govern your job. The standard terminology documents our industry uses give the baseline, but your employer's written practice and the applicable code have the final say. Get the definition right, then go look. Detect the indication, describe it accurately, and hand a clean record to whoever evaluates it. That discipline, more than any tool, is what makes a visual inspector worth trusting.

sections.heading: What Visual Testing Is
sections.body:
**Visual testing (VT)** is the examination of a material, component, or weld using light and the eye - unaided, or aided by simple optical tools and gauges, or relayed through remote devices such as borescopes and cameras - to evaluate its surface condition, geometry, and finish. If a feature can be seen, directly or through an optical aid, it falls within VT.

Two facts define the method's place in NDT. First, it is the **oldest** method - people judged the soundness of forgings and castings by eye for centuries before any instrument existed. Second, it is the **most widely applied** method - on most jobs VT is the first examination performed, and it is usually the only one applied to one hundred percent of the work, while other methods sample selected areas.

Because VT reads what reaches the surface, its strength is **surface-breaking and dimensional** condition: cracks open to the surface, undercut, porosity, corrosion, weld profile, fit-up, and finish. By itself it reveals nothing about subsurface volume; that is the job of radiographic and ultrasonic methods. Knowing that boundary keeps your conclusions honest and tells you when a second method is needed.

sections.title: VT detects and documents - it does not judge alone
sections.body: The core discipline of visual testing is to **find** a condition, **describe** it accurately, and **record** it. Whether that condition is acceptable is decided by evaluating it against the governing acceptance criteria - not by the inspector's opinion at the surface. Keep detection and judgment as two separate steps and you will rarely over-call or under-call.

sections.heading: A Short History of Inspection by Eye
sections.body:
For most of human history, quality control **was** visual. Smiths, founders, and shipwrights judged their work by eye, by the sound it rang with, and by feel. There was no other choice, and a skilled eye was a valuable trade asset.

The stakes rose sharply in the industrial age. Steam boilers, railroads, bridges, and pressure vessels could fail catastrophically, and boiler explosions in particular killed and injured many people. That hard experience drove the first written construction and inspection **codes** and, in time, the formal **qualification of inspectors** so that an examination meant the same thing from one shop to the next. In the 1960s the first widely adopted recommended practice for qualifying NDT personnel appeared, and VT was recognized as a method in its own right alongside radiography, penetrant, and magnetic particle.

The tools evolved in parallel. Simple **hand magnifiers and mirrors** extended the unaided eye; **rigid borescopes** let inspectors see inside bores and cylinders; **flexible fiber optics** reached around bends; **video borescopes** with electronic image sensors put the image on a screen; and today **robotic crawlers, drones, and digital cameras** carry the eye into places a person cannot go, with the record saved as data.

sections.caption: The evolution of visual testing, from judgment by eye through optical aids to today's remote and digital tools.

sections.heading: Where VT Is Used
sections.body:
Visual testing appears almost everywhere material is joined or a load is carried. The largest single use is **welding and fabrication**, where VT checks the base material, the joint preparation, the fit-up, the in-process passes, and the completed weld - often as a code requirement at each stage.

From there the list is broad. VT is front-line inspection for **castings and forgings**; for **pressure vessels, boilers, piping, and storage tanks** in both new construction and in-service work; and across the **power generation** sector, including turbines examined by borescope. The **oil and gas** industry relies on it for refineries, pipelines, and offshore structures. **Aerospace** depends heavily on VT, including borescope inspection of engine internals without teardown. It is essential to **bridges, cranes, and structural steel**, to **shipbuilding and marine** service, and to **railcars and automotive** manufacturing.

The common thread is that VT serves a part across its entire life - from incoming raw material, through every step of fabrication, and on through years of in-service examination. No other method is asked to cover so much ground so often.

sections.caption: Representative VT applications by sector.
rows: Joint prep, fit-up, in-process and final welds
Vessels, boilers, piping, tanks (new and in-service)

sections.heading: Core Terminology
sections.body:
The vocabulary of VT is precise, and using it loosely causes real errors. Learn these terms as a sequence:

- **Discontinuity** - any interruption in the normal physical structure or configuration of a part, such as a void, crack, inclusion, lap, or change in geometry. It is a neutral word; a discontinuity may or may not matter.
- **Indication** - the visible evidence that suggests a discontinuity is present. It is what you actually see and report.
- **Interpretation** - deciding what an indication truly represents: **relevant** (caused by a discontinuity), **nonrelevant** (caused by part geometry or condition, not a flaw), or **false** (an artifact, such as a scratch in dye or a smear).
- **Evaluation** - judging a relevant indication against the **acceptance criteria** in the code, specification, or procedure.
- **Defect** - a discontinuity that has been evaluated and found **rejectable**. The word defect always means rejectable.

Fix the central rule: a discontinuity is **not** automatically a defect. It becomes a defect only when evaluation against the acceptance criteria says it must be rejected. Blurring discontinuity and defect is the most common terminology mistake a new inspector makes.

sections.caption: The standard chain from a discontinuity to a defect, showing where interpretation and evaluation occur.

sections.title: Definitions live in your governing documents
sections.body: The exact meaning of a term can shift between industries and codes. Standard terminology documents used across NDT give a baseline, but the **procedure, specification, and your employer's written practice** that govern a given job carry the final definition. Always confirm how a term is defined for the work in front of you before you apply it.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best describes the scope of visual testing?
options: It detects subsurface volumetric flaws deep inside a part
It evaluates surface condition, geometry, and finish using light and the eye, with or without optical aids
It replaces radiographic and ultrasonic testing in all cases
It applies only to finished welds, never to raw material
checkQuestions.explanation: VT reads what reaches the surface - condition, geometry, and finish - using the eye aided by light, optical tools, or remote devices; it does not by itself reveal buried volumetric flaws.

checkQuestions.question: Why is visual testing considered both the oldest and the most widely used NDT method?
options: It is the only method that needs no light
People judged parts by eye for centuries, and today VT is usually first and applied to 100% of the work
It is the most expensive and therefore the most trusted
It is required only after all other methods are complete
checkQuestions.explanation: Judging soundness by eye predates every instrument, and on most jobs VT is the first examination and the only one performed on the entire piece, while other methods sample.

checkQuestions.question: A surface-breaking crack found during VT is correctly called a 'defect' only when:
options: It is first detected and recorded
It is visible without magnification
It has been evaluated against the acceptance criteria and found rejectable
A supervisor agrees it looks serious
checkQuestions.explanation: A discontinuity becomes a defect only after evaluation against the governing acceptance criteria determines it is rejectable; the word defect always means rejectable.

checkQuestions.question: An inspector sees a dark line in a weld and is not yet sure whether it is a crack, a geometry feature, or a smear of dirt. Determining which it is belongs to which step?
options: Evaluation
Interpretation
Acceptance
Documentation
checkQuestions.explanation: Interpretation is deciding what an indication truly represents - relevant, nonrelevant, or false - and comes before evaluating a relevant indication against acceptance criteria.

checkQuestions.question: Which of the following is the LEAST typical application of visual testing?
options: Checking weld fit-up and in-process passes during fabrication
Borescope inspection of aircraft engine internals
Measuring the depth of an internal flaw 30 mm below the surface of solid bar
In-service examination of bridge structural steel
checkQuestions.explanation: Sizing a flaw deep inside solid material is a volumetric task for UT or RT; VT covers surface and accessible internal surfaces, including borescope work, across fabrication and in-service inspection.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline 1.1-1.3 (definition, history, applications)
CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline 2.0 (definitions and standard terms)
ASTM E1316 standard terminology for NDT (general practice)
SNT-TC-1A / NAS410 (personnel qualification framework, general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (visual examination, general practice)
